source: RBI/2023-24/12 — Master Direction on Income Recognition, Asset Classification and Provisioning (IRACP)
text: As per RBI Master Direction dated April 2023, all regulated entities must ensure that the Fixed Obligation to Income Ratio (FOIR) does not exceed 50% for unsecured personal loans and 65% for secured home loans. FOIR is computed as the ratio of total fixed monthly obligations (including proposed EMI) to the net monthly income of the borrower. Lenders must verify income through Form 16, ITR, or bank statement salary credits before computing FOIR. Non-compliance with FOIR limits may result in higher provisioning requirements under IRACP norms.

source: RBI/2023-24/12 — FOIR Calculation Methodology
text: The FOIR computation must include all existing EMIs, credit card minimum dues, and other recurring obligations as disclosed by the applicant and verified through bureau records. For salaried individuals, net monthly income should be derived from the latest 3 months' salary slips or 6 months' bank statement credits. For self-employed applicants, the average monthly income from the last 2 years' ITR filings should be used. A FOIR exceeding 55% for any unsecured loan product is considered a regulatory BLOCK under RBI Household Income Guidelines 2023.

source: RBI Master Direction — Know Your Customer (KYC) Direction, 2016 (Updated 2023)
text: All regulated entities (REs) must complete Customer Due Diligence (CDD) before establishing any business relationship. PAN verification is mandatory for all financial transactions exceeding ₹50,000 as per Section 139A of the Income Tax Act. Aadhaar-based e-KYC is permitted under Section 11A of the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA). Both PAN and Aadhaar must be verified and linked as per CBDT Notification dated 30-03-2022. Failure to complete KYC verification is a mandatory BLOCK condition for loan disbursement.

source: RBI Master Direction — KYC Direction, 2016 — Simplified Due Diligence
text: For low-risk customers and small value accounts (balance not exceeding ₹50,000 and total credits not exceeding ₹1,00,000 in a year), simplified CDD measures may be applied. However, for loan products, full CDD including PAN and Aadhaar verification remains mandatory regardless of loan amount. The RE must verify the identity of the customer using the officially valid documents (OVDs) including passport, voter ID, driving license, PAN card, or Aadhaar card. At least two OVDs are required for loan applications.

source: RBI Housing Finance Circular 2024 — Loan-to-Value (LTV) Ratio Norms
text: For individual housing loans, the LTV ratio shall not exceed 80% for loan amounts up to ₹30 lakh and 75% for loan amounts above ₹30 lakh, as per RBI circular dated January 2024. LTV is calculated as the ratio of the loan amount to the market value of the property being financed. For properties under construction, the LTV should be calculated on the agreement value or the RBI-approved valuer's assessed value, whichever is lower. Non-compliance with LTV norms attracts higher risk weight (150% vs 50%) under Basel III capital adequacy framework.

source: RBI Vehicle Loan Guidelines 2022 — LTV Norms for Auto Loans
text: For vehicle loans (two-wheeler and four-wheeler), the Loan-to-Value ratio must not exceed 85% of the on-road price of the vehicle inclusive of insurance, registration, and road tax. For used vehicles, the LTV shall be computed on the depreciated value assessed by an approved valuer. Banks shall maintain documentation of vehicle valuation and insurance assignment as collateral security. Auto loans exceeding 85% LTV require additional collateral or a higher interest rate spread to compensate for elevated risk.

source: RBI Retail Lending Master Direction 2023 — Applicant Eligibility
text: The applicant must be a minimum of 21 years of age at the time of loan application and shall not exceed 65 years at the time of loan maturity. For joint applications, the primary applicant's age is considered for eligibility determination. Banks may apply stricter age criteria based on internal credit policies, but they cannot relax the 21-65 age band prescribed by RBI. Age verification must be done through OVDs such as PAN card, Aadhaar, or passport with date of birth clearly mentioned.

source: RBI Credit Information Companies (Regulation) Act, 2005 — Bureau Check Mandate
text: All banks and financial institutions regulated by RBI must mandatorily obtain a credit information report from at least one RBI-licensed Credit Information Company (CIC) before sanctioning any credit facility. The four licensed CICs are: TransUnion CIBIL, Equifax, Experian, and CRIF High Mark. A bureau check must be performed within 30 days prior to the date of loan sanction. The credit report must be maintained as part of the loan file for audit purposes. Failure to perform bureau check is a regulatory non-compliance under the CICRA 2005.

source: RBI Master Circular on Frauds — Classification and Reporting 2023
text: As per RBI Master Circular on Frauds dated July 2023, all Regulated Entities must maintain an internal fraud registry and cross-check every loan applicant's PAN against the Central Fraud Registry maintained by RBI. Applications from PANs flagged in the fraud registry must be mandatorily rejected. The RE must report any new fraud above ₹1 lakh to RBI within 21 days of detection. Internal fraud databases must be updated within 7 working days of fraud confirmation. PAN blacklist checks are a BLOCK condition for loan origination.

source: Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA) / RBI AML Guidelines 2023
text: Under PMLA and RBI Master Direction on KYC, regulated entities must conduct enhanced due diligence for transactions that are unusual or complex in nature. For personal loans exceeding ₹10,00,000 (ten lakh rupees), the borrower must provide a declaration of source of funds and intended use. Suspicious Transaction Reports (STRs) must be filed with the Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU-IND) within 7 days. AML screening includes checking the applicant against UNSC sanctions lists, OFAC SDN list, and domestic law enforcement databases.

source: RBI Master Direction on KYC — AML Risk Categorization
text: Regulated entities must classify customers into Low, Medium, and High risk categories based on parameters such as customer identity, social/financial status, nature of business activity, and country of origin. High-risk customers (including Politically Exposed Persons) require enhanced CDD including senior management approval, source of funds verification, and ongoing enhanced monitoring. For loan products, AML risk categorization must be completed before credit committee approval.

source: Internal Credit Policy ICP-2024-07 — Income Documentation Standards
text: Income proof documentation (Form 16, salary slips, ITR, bank statements) must not be older than 24 months from the date of loan application. For salaried applicants, the most recent 3 months' salary slips and latest Form 16 are considered valid income proof. For self-employed applicants, ITR for the last 2 completed assessment years is required. Income proof older than 24 months is flagged as a WARNING condition requiring updated documentation before final disbursement. This aligns with RBI's guidance on income verification for retail lending.

source: RBI Fair Practices Code — Retail Lending 2023
text: As per RBI Fair Practices Code, all loan applications must be processed within a reasonable timeframe and the applicant must be informed of the decision (approval, rejection, or request for additional information) within 15 working days. The reason for rejection must be communicated in writing. Interest rates must be transparently communicated and linked to an external benchmark (repo rate or T-bill rate). Prepayment charges are prohibited for floating rate loans. Any fees or charges must be disclosed upfront in the loan agreement.

source: RBI Guidelines on Digital Lending — 2022
text: Digital lending platforms must adhere to RBI guidelines issued on September 2, 2022. All loan disbursements and repayments must be directly between the RE's bank account and the borrower's bank account without any pass-through or pool account. The borrower must be provided a Key Fact Statement (KFS) containing the Annual Percentage Rate (APR), loan tenure, EMI amount, and total interest cost before loan execution. Digital lenders must provide a cooling-off period of minimum 3 days during which the borrower can exit the loan without penalty.

source: RBI Master Direction — Risk Weight for Retail Exposures
text: Under Basel III capital adequacy framework adopted by RBI, retail loan exposures carry risk weights based on LTV and product type. Housing loans up to ₹30 lakh with LTV ≤ 80% carry 35% risk weight. Housing loans above ₹75 lakh carry 50% risk weight. Personal loans and unsecured exposures carry 100% risk weight. Consumer credit (credit cards) with overdue carry 150% risk weight. Higher risk weights directly impact the bank's Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR) and lending capacity.

source: RBI Circular on NPA Recognition — 90 Days Past Due Norm
text: A loan account is classified as Non-Performing Asset (NPA) if the principal or interest payment remains overdue for a period of 90 days or more. For agricultural loans, the NPA classification period is one or two crop seasons depending on the crop. RBI mandates automated NPA classification through the Asset Classification and Provisioning system. Borrowers with existing NPA accounts at any bank (as reflected in the credit bureau report) require enhanced scrutiny including a detailed write-up on why fresh credit is being considered.

source: RBI Circular — Delinquency and DPD Reporting Standards
text: All regulated entities must report Days Past Due (DPD) information to Credit Information Companies on a monthly basis. DPD data includes: DPD 0 (current), DPD 1-30 (SMA-0), DPD 31-60 (SMA-1), DPD 61-90 (SMA-2), and DPD 90+ (NPA). Multiple instances of DPD 90+ in the last 24 months are considered a significant negative credit signal. Applicants with 2 or more DPD 90+ instances should be subjected to enhanced credit scrutiny including manual review by a senior credit officer. DPD information must be accurately maintained for a minimum of 36 months in the credit bureau records.

source: RBI Guidelines on EMI Bounce — Impact on Creditworthiness
text: EMI bounces (dishonour of ECS/NACH mandates) are considered a significant indicator of cash flow stress. Regulated entities must track and report EMI bounce data to credit bureaus. Three or more EMI bounces in the trailing 12 months are considered a hard rule fraud/risk signal. Banks may charge bounce charges as per their published schedule of charges (capped at ₹750 per instance as per recent RBI directive). Repeated EMI bounces may lead to reclassification of the account from Standard to Special Mention Account (SMA).

source: RBI Master Direction — Credit Bureau Enquiry Norms
text: Each hard enquiry (loan application) is recorded by the Credit Information Company and remains on the credit report for 24 months. Multiple hard enquiries within a short period (more than 4 in 6 months) indicate credit-hungry behaviour and are flagged as a negative signal by most credit scoring models. Soft enquiries (self-check, pre-approved offers) do not impact the credit score. Regulated entities should consider the number of recent enquiries as part of their credit assessment framework. An enquiry spike may also indicate identity theft or fraudulent applications.

source: RBI Circular on Loan Product Limits — Unsecured Lending Cap 2023
text: RBI has directed banks to increase the risk weight on unsecured consumer credit (excluding education and housing) to 125% from 100%, effective November 2023. This circular aims to curb excessive growth in unsecured retail lending. Banks must ensure that their unsecured loan portfolio does not exceed a board-approved internal limit as a percentage of total advances. Personal loan interest rates must factor in the higher capital cost due to increased risk weights. This regulation impacts the bank's portfolio-level risk appetite for personal loans.

source: RBI Circular — Name Match Verification for KYC Compliance
text: The name on the loan application must match the name recorded in the KYC documents (PAN, Aadhaar) within an acceptable tolerance. Regulated entities must implement name matching algorithms with a minimum threshold of 80% match score. Minor variations in spelling, middle name omission, or transliteration differences should be handled through fuzzy matching. A name match score below 80% requires manual verification by a KYC officer. Name mismatches between PAN and Aadhaar should be flagged for additional document verification before loan sanction.

source: RBI Guidelines on Debt-to-Income (DTI) Assessment
text: Banks should assess the borrower's total debt burden including the proposed loan against their gross income. The DTI ratio (total monthly debt payments divided by gross monthly income) should generally not exceed 50% for retail loans. Unlike FOIR which uses net income, DTI considers gross income and includes all types of debt obligations. Banks are expected to use both FOIR and DTI as complementary measures for credit assessment. An elevated DTI ratio (above 45%) combined with a high FOIR (above 50%) should trigger enhanced scrutiny.

source: RBI Master Direction — Stress Testing Framework for Retail Lending
text: Regulated entities must conduct periodic stress tests on their retail loan portfolio under various macroeconomic scenarios including: (1) income stress — assuming 20% reduction in borrower income, (2) interest rate stress — assuming 200 basis points increase in lending rates, (3) unemployment stress — assuming sector-specific job loss scenarios. Individual loan affordability must be tested against at least the base and moderate stress scenarios before approval. Loans that fail the moderate stress test (FOIR exceeding 65% under stress) should be flagged for enhanced review.

source: RBI Circular — Macro-Prudential Framework for Retail Credit
text: The macroeconomic stress scenario configuration (NORMAL, MILD_STRESS, HIGH_STRESS) must be periodically reviewed and updated based on RBI's Financial Stability Report. Under HIGH_STRESS scenario, sector-specific NPA rate multipliers are applied to the computed Probability of Default. Banks must maintain adequate Capital Conservation Buffer (CCB) of 2.5% over and above the minimum CAR of 9% during stress periods. Stress-adjusted PD values must be used for provisioning calculations under Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS 109).

source: RBI Guidelines — IP and Digital Fraud Prevention in Lending
text: Regulated entities offering digital lending products must implement IP-based risk scoring to detect VPN/proxy usage, geographic anomalies, and bot-driven applications. Applications originating from high-risk IP addresses (VPN, Tor, data center IPs) must be flagged for enhanced verification. Country mismatch between the applicant's declared address and IP geolocation is a fraud indicator. Form fill time analysis (applications completed in under 45 seconds suggest automated/bot submissions) should be part of the digital fraud detection framework.
